A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

ATTORNEYS AT LAW

DONALD A. JACKSON

ELIOT S. NAHIGIAN
HARRY E. MACY

ROBERT J. PYLE
JOHN H. MISSIRLIAN
CRAIG A. DAVIS
NEAL E. COSTANZO
LISA JORGENSEN NILMEIER

*CERTIFIED TAX SPECIALIST

7108 NORTH FRESNO STREET
FOURTH FLOOR

FRESNO, CALIFORNIA 93720-2938

POST OFFICE BOX 28400
FRESNO, CALIFORNIA 93729-8400

FACSIMILE: 209/26-0910
TELEPHONE: 209/26-0800

February 19, 1991


File No. 7070.04


Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: Conflict of Interest Issues

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     Please be advised that this office represents the Wasco
Redevelopment Agency ("WRDA").

     On behalf of our client, we are requesting formal advice
regarding various conflict of interest issues and the procedures
that should be followed. The factual information is contained in
the letter of January 22, 1991, and the follow-up Memorandum of
January 23, 1991 from WRDA, both of which are attached hereto. I
have also enclosed for your reference, a copy of the Memorandum of
January 24, 1991, prepared by Tom Keene of this office in response
to the request by WRDA.

     On behalf of our client, we are hereby requesting a
formal written opinion from the FPPC regarding the two cases as
outlined in the correspondence from WRDA. It is my understanding
that it may take up to 3 weeks to receive the FPPC opinion.

     If there is further information required, please do not
hesitate to contact Tom Keene or myself.

Very truly yours,

JACKSON, HARGROVE, HILLISON & EMERICH

[signature]

John H. Missirlian

JACKSON, HARGROVE, HILLISON & EMERICH
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

7108 NORTH FRESNO STREET POST OFFICE BOX 28400

DONALD A. JACKSON*
RICHARD H. HARGROVE
RONALD E. HILLISON
DAVID R. EMERICH
THOMAS A. PEDREIRA
ELIOT S. NAHIGIAN
HARRY E. MACY
JAMES R. LUPPINO
DAVID A. FIKE
THOMAS J. FORD
JOHN H. MISSIRLAN
CRAIG A. DAVIS
NEAL E. COSTANZO
LISA JORGENSEN NILMEIER
JEFFREY S. SMALL

*CERTIFIED TAX SPECIALIST

April 24, 1991

File No. 7070.04

Mark Morodomi, Esq.
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J. Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0807
Fax: (916) 327-2026

Re: Letter No. 91-109

Dear Mr. Morodomi:

We appreciated very much your prompt response to our
request for an opinion regarding the above-referenced matter.

When Tom Keene and I spoke with you several months ago,
you indicated you would follow up your oral opinion with a written
opinion. To date, we have not received a copy of the written
opinion. Unfortunately, our client needs this opinion rather
urgently.

Is it possible to fax that opinion to me by Friday, April
26, 1991? If not, how soon thereafter can we obtain a copy of that
opinion?

Please advise at your earliest convenience.

Thank you for your consideration in this matter.

Sincerely,

JACKSON, HARGROVE, HILLISON & EMERICH

[signature]

John H. Missirlan

JHM:abg
cc: WASCO Redevelopment Agency
707004JM.L03

SENT VIA FACSIMILE AND MAILED
ON 04/24/91

WRDA WASCO REDEVELOPMENT AGENCY
746 8th STREET P.O. BOX 159 WASCO, CA 93280 (805) 758-3003

January 22, 1991

Mr. Richard Hargrove
Attorney-At-Law
Jackson, Hargrove, Hillison & Emerick
7108 North Fresno Ave.
Fresno, California 93720

RE: Questions on Conflict of Interest

Dear Dick:

There have arisen two situations where RDA Board
Members have or may have interest in two projects before the
RDA. Please advise us as to what these Board Members should
do, should not do, or may do with regards to these two
projects.

Case #1

There is a 4-plex being built at 5th & D Streets in
Wasco. There is a partnership which is financing and
building this apartment. Being in the WRDA Project Area,
they have asked for a $5,000 - $8,000 financial assistance
grant for either excavation, or for sidewalks and driveway
improvements. One of the partners is Board Member Fred
West, Jr. He has not participated in any discussions of the
project. He will even leave the Board Chambers when the
matter is discussed. However, other board members believe
they may also have conflicts of interest. One members
nephew-in-law is another partner in the project. Another
Board Member sold West the land. And another member owns
property next door. So 4 of the 5 Board Members are
concerned about their conflicts-of-interest.

The questions about the above are this:

1) May the Board take any action on any item or
project where one of their fellow members has a
direct ownership interest? If so, what procedures
must they follow in dealing with the matter?

2) As I have described it, do the Board members who
do not have a direct interest in the project, have
valid conflicts of interest anyway? If not, what
should they do in resolving this issue?

3)     Are there any other standards or rules the Board
       ought to be aware of when confronting this type of
       situation?

Case #2

     This involves a development by two eye physicians.
They had already received their permit to build when they
approached the RDA to assist in a particularly unusual
problem. The phone company, Pac Bell, said that in order to
move a line pole at the Street which was blocking placement
of a driveway, Pac Bell would have to be paid in advance by
the owners. It will cost over $8,000 to move the pole. The
driveway could only be placed at the designated location to
make the lot a legally usable lot. The owners asked if the
RDA could make the payment through a grant as they had
already received their development loan.

     The possible conflict-of-interest problem is that the
building contractor they hired to construct the building is
Board Member Paul Neufeld. Mr. Neufeld has never intervened
in the discussions with the physicians. He has never
represented them to the City or RDA officials. The
replacement of the pole is not a part of Mr. Neufelds
contract for services and will not effect his contract one
way or another.

     The question about conflicts-of-interest in this case
are:

     1)     Should Board Member Neufeld remove himself from
            the meeting in which this matter is discussed?

     2)     Again, can this matter even be acted upon given
            Directors Neufeld's contract with the owners?

     3)     Are there any other standards or rules the Board
            ought to be aware of when confronting this type of
            situation?

     Richard, I hope my explanations of the cases are
thorough enough to be helpful. Of course, we would like
some guidance as soon as possible. Please let me know if
you have any questions.

     Sincerely yours,

     [signature]
     John F. Hendrickson
     City Manager

WRDA WASCO REDEVELOPMENT AGENCY
746 8th STREET P. O. BOX 159 WASCO, CA 93280 (805) 758-3003

January 23, 1991

MEMORANDUM

TO : Richard Hargrove

FROM : John Hendrickson

SUBJECT: Amendments to "Conflict of Interest" letter

I sent you a letter concerning possible conflicts of interest involving RDA
members. I am including two amendments to that letter.

The amendments are:

1) Case # 1, 1st paragraph, 11th line, the sentence should read,
"...nephew-in-law, once removed,..."

2) Case # 1, 1st paragraph, 12th line, the sentence should read,
"...sold West the land three (3) years ago when neither was on the
Board or City Council."

I hope these additions will make the facts more accurate.

MEMORANDUM

DATE:     January 24, 1991

FROM:     Tom Keene, Office of General Counsel to the Redevelopment
          Agency

TO:       John Hendrickson, City Manager

RE:       Your recent correspondence inquiring about possible
          conflict of interest issues


Case # 1

     As I understand this matter, a partnership which is
developing a multi-family housing unit in the project area has
asked the Agency for financial assistance. (A) One of the partners
is a member of the Agency's Board. (B) One of the other members of
the Agency's Board has a nephew-in-law, once removed, who is
another one of the partners. (C) A third member of the Board had
owned this same property but had sold it to member A three years
ago when neither was on the Board or the City Council.
(D) A fourth board member owns land which is next door to the
property which is the subject of the development.

1)     May the Board take any action on any item or project where one
       of their fellow members has a direct ownership interest? If
       so, what procedures must they follow in dealing with the
       matter?

       Short answer: Yes, the board members who do not,
themselves, have a direct ownership interest may participate in
decision-making with regard to the property, unless otherwise
prohibited by law.

       Discussion: The mere fact one or two of their members
has a conflict of interest prohibits only that member from
participating in the decision. Members who do not have conflict
of interest themselves may participate. No special procedures must
be followed. However, if more than one Board member has a conflict
then the issue is whether there are sufficient members on the Board
who do not have a conflict of interest to constitute a quorum of
the Board. If a quorum remains, then that quorum can decide¹. If

___________________
     ¹In other words, we do not count the conflicted member for any
purpose, including the establishment of a quorum. For example, if
a five member board had two members declare a conflict of interest
and step down, and one of the other three members was no present
that night, the remaining two, non-conflicted members cannot act on
this agenda item because they lost their quorum when the two who
had conflicts of interest declared them. The rule of necessity
(continued...)

FILE COPY

John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
Page 2

a quorum would not remain if all of those with conflicts step down,
then the rule of necessity' says all of the members, including
those with conflicts of interest, (whether direct or indirect), may
participate in the decision after having made their oral disclosure
of the conflict of interest.

2)    As described above, do the Board members who do not have a
direct interest in the project, have valid conflicts of
interest anyway?  If not, what should they do in resolving
this issue?

Short answer:  Board members A and D have a conflict of
interest but Board members B and C do not. If B and C continue to
maintain that they have a conflict of interest then no decision can
be made. The rule of necessity does not apply. If they agree that
they do not, then B, C and the other members of the Board, (other
than A and D), may act.  However, please read the answer to (3)
below before taking any action.

Discussion:  In our opinion, based on the following
discussion, Board member A, who is himself a partner, has a
conflict of interest in making a decision as to whether to lend the
partnership money to improve the real property and, so long as he

________________

'(...continued)
would not help under this set of facts, because there are
sufficient board members in office to make a decision, if they were
all present. Therefore, there is no choice but to continue the
matter.

²This rule is the product of FPPC decisions, Attorney General
Opinions and some court decisions. It is based on a section of the
Fair Political Practices Act which says that the Act,
"does not prevent any public official from making or
participating in the making of a governmental decision to
the extent his participation is legally required for the
decision to be made. The fact that an official's vote is
needed to break a tie does not make his participation
legally required for purposes of this section."
The regulations then interpret this to mean that the rule of
necessity applies if and only if, "there exists no alternative
source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the
statute authorizing the decision."  The regulation goes on to
specify that this rule is not to be construed to permit an official
who has a conflict of interest to vote merely to break a tie nor
permit him to vote,
"if a quorum can be convened of other members of the
agency who are not disqualified (under the Act) whether
or not such other members are actually present at the
time of disqualification."

John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
Page 3

is either a partner in a business which owns land in the Project
Area or owns land in the Project Area directly, will have conflicts
of interest with regard to many of the major decisions involving the
redevelopment project. Board member B, probably has no conflict of
interest merely because of the partnership status of the Board
member's nephew-in-law, once removed. If Board member C has been
paid for the property in question and has no remaining legal
interest in the property, then Board member C probably has no
conflict of interest either. Board member D has a conflict of
interest only if the loan to A's partnership would have will have
"no financial effect" whatsoever on D's property. Since the loan
to A's partnership will be used to develop the partnership's land,
it is clear that the action will have some financial effect on the
value of D's land. Therefore, D has a conflict of interest.

The term "conflict of interest" is most easily defined by
the Fair Political Practices Act. Section 87100 of the Government
Code provides:

"No public official at any level of state or local
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest."

The term "public official at any level of state or local
government," is defined in the regulations which are found at Title
2, California Administrative Code, Section 18700, et. seq. The
term is defined to mean, "every natural person who is a member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency." Clearly a city council member or a member of the Board of
Directors of a Redevelopment Agency is a "public official" within
this definition.

The term, "makes a governmental decision" is defined to
include voting, appointing a person, entering into a contract on
behalf of the agency or "obligates or commits his or her agency to
any course of action." It can also include a decision not to take
any of these actions, unless the decision not to act is made
because of the financial interest. "When the determination not to
act occurs because of his or her financial interest, the official's
determination must be accompanied by disclosure of the financial
interest, made part of the agency's official record or made in
writing to the, . ." to the appropriate person. Again, any formal
action by the Board of the Redevelopment Agency which affects
property would probably be considered the making of a governmental
decision.

The term "attempting to use his official position to
influence", is defined in the regulations to include, "for the
purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts, or

John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
Page 4


appears before, or other wise attempts to influence, any member,
officer, employee or consultant of the agency," in which he is an
official or over which the agency in which he is an official has
budgetary control.

"Attempting to influence", also includes "appearance or
contact by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer." So, in other words, if Board member A, who is a member
of the partnership which owns the property were to even contact
employees or other Board members on behalf of the partnership, he
would be "attempting to use his official position [with the Agency]
to influence" a decision which the Agency was making in regard to
the property. The language of this regulation is so broad that
many attorneys advise the affected public official to at least step
down from the dais where the other members are discussing the
matter and some even advise the person to leave the room entirely
in order to avoid the appearance of "attempting to influence" the
decision.

The term "material financial effect" is defined in the
regulations based on the nature of the interest.

Boardmember A: Under the regulations adopted pursuant to
the Fair Political Practices Act, the decision is said to have a
material financial effect if "any business entity in which the
official is. . .[a] partner. . .is directly involved in a decision
before the official's agency. If the official owned land
directly, then the decision is said to have a material financial
effect if,

"the decision is to . . . adopt the redevelopment plan,
to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind
or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in
which the official has an interest, or any part of it is
located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area."

Similarly, if the official owned the land directly involved in the
decision, then if the decision involved the imposition or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed on the property or if
the decision involved the revocation or denial of a license or
permit or land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of the
property then the decision clearly has a material financial effect
on the property.

Boardmember B: The general rule contained in the
regulations in regard to whether a decision has a "material
financial effect", is that the decision does have such an effect,

"if the decision will have a significant effect on
the official or a member of the official's immediate

John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
Page 5

family, or on the source of income, the source of gifts,
the business entity, or the real property, which is an
economic interest of the official."

Additional provisions state that the effect of a decision is
material if that decision will result in the

"personal expenses, income, assets (other than interest
in real property), or liabilities of the official or his
or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by at
least $250."

Repeatedly, the regulations, when they refer to anyone other than
the official himself or herself, the reference is to "his or her
immediate family." Unfortunately, the regulations never define who
is within an "immediate family". The fact that the adjective
"immediate" was used tends to mean to me that someone as remote as
a nephew-in-law, once removed, was not intended to be included.
Certainly if the people in question are adults who do not reside in
the same household, this remote a relationship would not come
within the scope of the term "immediate family", in my opinion.

Boardmember D: You do not say what kind of property
Board member D owns. If it is his residence then one set of rules
apply. If it is not his residence then another set applies.
First, I will assume that it was not his residence:

A decision is said to have a "material financial effect"
on property indirectly involved in the decision if the effect of
the decision is material as to real property which the official has
a direct interest in and, one of the following applies (a) the
property is within a 300 foot radius of the boundaries of the
property which is the subject of the decision, "unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the official's real property
interest". or (b) the real property is outside of the 300 foot
radius but within a 2,500 foot radius and the decision will have a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 or more on the
market value of the official's land, or of $1,000 or more in annual
rental value of the official's land. If the land is more than
2,500 feet from the subject property then the decision is presumed
by law to have no material financial effect on the official's
property.

If the property is D's residence then a special rule
applies. Because the City has a population of less than 25,000
people and covers a geographic area of ten square miles, or less,
and the council members are required to reside in the jurisdiction,
and, are elected at large and we are talking about an indirect
benefit to D's property, then we can ignore the 2,500 foot radius
issue. However, the 300 foot radius would still apply. Since you

John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
Page 6


say that D's property is "next door", I assume that it is less than
300 feet away.

3)    Are there any other standards or rules the Board ought to be
      aware of when confronting this type of situation?

      Short answer: Yes.

      Discussion: All of the above analysis is based on the
Fair Political Practices Act and the regulations adopted pursuant
to it. There are, however, two other sources of binding law and
regulations on the subject of conflicts of interest. First of all,
other provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act require the
City to have adopted its own conflict of interest code. Some codes
adopted by cities merely recite provisions of the Act, other have
their own substantive rules. I do not know if your City or the
Agency by itself have adopted any such code.

      Secondly, the Government Code contains another chapter on
conflicts of interests which takes a very different approach.
Section 1090 of the Government Code provides that,

      "members of the . . .district,. . . and city officers or
      employees shall not be financially interested in any
      contract made by them in their official capacity, or by
      any body or board of which they are members."

It then defines, "district" to include any

      "agency of the state formed pursuant to general law or
      special act, for the local performance of governmental or
      proprietary functions within limited boundaries."

In our opinion, this provision applies to members of the Board of
a Redevelopment Agency. The only exception to this broad rule is
laid out in Section 1091, which says that an officer of the entity
is not considered to be interested in a contract entered into by
the District if the officer has only a remote interest in the
contract and, "if the fact of that interest is disclosed to the
body of the Board of which the officer is a member and noted in its
official records" and the Board then approves the contract. It
then goes on for thirteen separate sub-sections which define
"remote interest". None of these definitions seem to apply to
Board member A.

      If Section 1090 applies to Board member A, then there is
no escape from its effect. Unlike provisions of the Fair Political
Practices Act, in which disqualification from participation in
decisions on that contract would meet the requirements of the code
provisions, Section 1090 does not have an easy method of complying
with the law. Under this statutory scheme, the contract would be

John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
Page 7

voidable. The only way of avoiding this effect would be for either
(a) Board member A to resign from the Board or (b) sell his
interest in the partnership to a non-board member or (c) for the
partnership simply not to apply for the loan.

Case # 2

Two eye physicians have received their permit to build.
They were then told by Pac Bell that, in order to move a telephone
pole which was blocking placement of the driveway to the building,
Pac Bell would have to be paid in advance by the doctors. This
operation will cost over $8,000. The doctors have asked the Agency
to grant them the money to move the pole. The doctor's general
contractor on the project is Board member Neufeld. The moving of
the pole will actually be performed by someone other than Neufeld
or his employees and the decision of the Agency in regard to the
grant will have no effect on Neufeld's contract.

1) Should Board member Neufeld remove himself from the meeting in
which this matter is discussed?

Short answer: It would probably be prudent but it may
not be required.

Discussion: Section 1090 of the Government Code applies
only to financial interest which Board members may have in
contracts made by the Agency. This would not appear to apply in
this situation. The Fair Political Practices Act applies only to
contracts in which the public official has a material financial
interest. As set forth in your statement of facts, Board member
Neufeld has no material financial interest in the contract between
the Agency and the doctors.

However, the regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission provide that the effect of a decision is material if,

"Any person (including a business entity) which has been
a source of income . . . to the official of $250 or more
in the preceding 12 months is directly involved in a
decision before the official's agency or there is a nexus
[as defined below] between the purpose for which the
official receives income and the governmental decision.
. . . There is a nexus between the purpose for which an
official receives income and a governmental decision if
the official receives income to achieve a goal or purpose
which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by
the decision."

Arguably, in this case, Neufeld received income to achieve the goal
of bringing the construction of the building to a point where it
could be occupied. The decision of the Agency to grant the money

John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
Page 8


to the physicians would aide in bringing the construction of the
building to a point where it could be occupied. Admittedly this is
a rather broad reading of the regulation. However, I have seen the
FPPC read their regulations even more broadly than this.

     Since, in this case, we are talking about only one member
of the Board being disqualified to act and, if he does not step
down and the FPPC later determines that he should have, possible
criminal penalties could be applied, then I would suggest erring on
the side of caution. Additionally, I assume that Mr. Neufeld has
been paid at least $250 by the doctors within the last 12 months.
Therefore, if the first part of the above-quoted provision is read
separately, then the decision whether to grant the money is clearly
material. This analysis would serve further to warrant caution.

2)   Again, can this matter even be acted upon given Director
     Neufeld's contract with the owners?

     Short answer: Yes.

     Discussion: Because we are dealing only with the Fair
Political Practices Act and not with Section 1090 of the Government
Code, drastic measures are not required. Board member Neufeld can
step down and not participate in making the decision. This would
leave more than enough members of the Board without a conflict of
interest who could then take action. If enough of the others also
had valid conflicts of interest so as to keep the Agency from being
able to have a quorum of Board members with no conflict of interest
then, the rule of necessity says all of the members of the Board,
including Mr. Neufeld, come back in and can act, so long as they
make an oral disclosure of their conflict of interest before
participating in the decision making process.

3)   Are there any other standards or rules the Board ought to be
     aware of when confronting this type of situation?

     Short answer: Yes.

     Discussion: As noted above, the Board or the City should
have adopted its own conflict of interest code and should keep that
code up to date. That may have some relevant material in it.
Secondly, with this type of issue, different businesses or
professions have their own sets of rules with regard to conflicts
of interest which may or may not be applicable. For example,
building contractors are licensed by the State of California and
are heavily regulated. If they have a code of professional
conduct, it may prohibit Director Neufeld from acting both as
general contractor for the physicians and then acting as member of
the Board of the Redevelopment Agency in deciding whether to lend
them enough money to finish out the same project which he helped
build.

John Hendrickson, City Manager
January 24, 1991
Page 9


        John, I hope that the above information is not too filled
with legalisms to be understandable.  Obviously this is a very
complex set of questions.  If I can shed any more light on it
please do not hesitate to give me a call and we can discuss each
aspect of these cases.

H:707004TK.M01

In our opinion, based on the following

The regulations then interpret this to mean that the rule of

The regulation goes on to

The term "conflict of lnterest" is nost easily defined by

"No public officiat at any 1eveI of state or loca]
government shall nake, participate in makinq or ln any

The tern "pubtic officlal at any level of state or 1oca1
government'i, is defined 1n the legulatlons t{hich are found at TitIe
2, california Administratlve code, Seqtion 18?00, et' seq. The
tern is defined to mean, "every natural person i,ho is a menber,

The tern, "nakes a govelnmental decision" is defined to

The term "attemptinq to use his official position to
influence", is defined in the regulations to include, "for Lhe
purpose of influencing the decision, the offlclal contacts, or

appears before, or other wise attempts to influence, any member,
officer, 
employee or consultant of the agency," in which he is an
official 
or over which the agency in which he is an official 
has
budgetary control.
"Attempting to influence", also includes "aPpearance or
contact by the official 
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer. " So, in other words, if Board member A, who is a nember
of the partnership which own the property were to even contact
empLoyees or other Board members on behalf of the partnership, he
would be "attempting to use his official position Iwith the Agency]
to influence" a decision which the Agency was making in regard to
the property. 
The language of this regulation is so broad that
many attorneys advise the affected public official to at least step
down from the dias where the other members are discussing the
matter and some even advise the person to leave the room entirely
in order to avoid the appearance of "attempting to influence" the
decis ion.
The term "material financial effect" is defined in the
regulations based on the nature of the interest'
Boardmember A: Under the regulations adopted Pursuant to
the Fair P6ITEGI Practices Act, the decision is said to have a
material financial effect if "any business entity in which the
officlal 
is. 
.Ia] partner. 
.is directly involved in a decision
before the officiat's 
agency." 
If the official 
owned land
directly, then the decision is said to have a material financial
effect if ,
"the decision is to 
adopt the redevelopnent plan,
to add terrltory to the redevelopment area, or to rescind
or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in
which the official 
has an interest, or any part of it is
located within 
the boundaries (or 
the 
proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopment area."
Similarly, if the official 
owned the land directly involved in the
decision, then if 
the decision involved the imposition or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed on the property or if
the decision involved the revocation or denial of a license or
permit or land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of the
property then the decision clearly has a material financial effect
on the property.
Boardmember B:
The general rule contained in the
regulations in regard to whether a decision has a "material
financial effect", is that the decision does have such an effect,
"if the decision will have a significant effect on
the official 
or a member of the official's 
immediate

January 24, I99 L
family, or on the source of income, the source of gifts,
the business entity, or the real property, which is an

"personal expenses, income, assets (other than interest
in real property), or liabilities 
of the official 
or his
or her immediate family increasing or decreasing by at
least $250. "
Repeatedly, the regulations, when they refer to anyone other than
the official 
himself or herself, the reference is to "his or her
immediate family. " Unfortunately, the regulations never define vrho
is within an "immediate famiIy". 
The fact that the adJective
"immediate" was used tends to mean to me that someone as remote as
a nephe!,r- in- Iar,r, once renoved, was not intended to be included.
Certainly if the people in question are adults who do not reside in
the same household, this remote a relationship would not come

appIy. If it is not his
You do not say what kind of ProPerty

residence then another set applies.
First, I will assume that it $ras not his residence:
A decision is said to have a "material financiaf effect"
on property indirectty involved in the decision lf the effect of

radius but within a 2,500 foot radius and the decision wiII have a
reasonably foreseeable financial effect of $10,000 more on the

rental value of the official's land. If the land is more than

If the property is D's residence then a special rule

3) 
Are there any other standards or rules the Board ought to be
aware of when confronting this type of situation?
Short answer: Yes.
Discussion: All of the above analysis is based on the
fair politIEEI practices Act and the regulations adopted pursuant
to it. 
There are, however, two other sources of binding 1aw and
regul-ations on the subject of conflicts of interest. First of aI1,
other provisions of the Fair Political Practices Act require the
City to have adopted its ovrn conflict of j.nterest code. Some codes
adopted by cities merely recite provisions of the Act, other have
their own substantive rules. I do not know if your City or the
Agency by itself have adopted any such code.
Secondly, the Government Code contains another chapter on
conflicts of interests which takes a very different approach.
Section 1090 of the Government Code Provides that,
"members of the . 
.district,. . . and city officers or
employees shall not be financially lnterested in any
contract made by them ln their official capacity, or by
any body or board of which they are rnembers. "
It then defines, "district" to include any
"agency of the state formed pursuant to general law or
special act, for the local performance of governmental or
proprietary functlons within limited boundaries."
In our opinion, this provision applies to members of the Board of
a Redevel.opment Agency. The only exception to this broad rule is
Iaid out in Section 1091, which says that an officer of the entity
is not considered to be interested in a contract entered into by
the District if the officer has only a remote interest in the
contract and, "if the fact of that interest is disclosed to the
body of the Board of which the officer is a member and noted in its
official records" and the Board then approves the contract. It
then goes on for thirteen separate sub-sections which define
"remote interest". 
None of these definitions seem to appty to
Board member A.
If Section 1090 applies to Board member A, then there is
no escape from its effect, Unlike provisions of the Fair political
Practices Act, in which di squal i f ication from participation in
decisions on that contract would meet the reguirements oi the code
provisions, Section 1090 does not have an easy method of complying
lrith the law. Under this statutory scheme, the contract woufa Ue

January 24, l99L l

. * ,'tn*
,J"*vl'"1
voidable. {he only way of avoiding this effect wou}d be for either
(a) Board member A to resign from the Board or (b) sell his
interest in the partnership to a non-board member or (c) for the
partnership simply not to apply for the Ioan.
Case # 2
Two eye physicians have received their permit to buiId.
They then were told by Pac BeII that, in order to move a telephone
pole which was blocking placement of the driveway to the building,
Pac BelI would have to be paid in advance by the doctors. 
This
operation wiII cost over $8,000. The doctors have asked the Agency

The doctor's general
contractor on the project is Board member Neufeld. The moving of
the pole will actually be performed by someone other than Neufeld
or hls employees and the decision of the Agency in regard to the
grant wiII have no effect on Neufeld's contract.
1) 
Should Board member Neufeld remove himself from the meeting in
which this matter is discussed?
Short ansr"rer I It would probably be prudent but it may
not be req[irea. 
-
Discussion: Section 1090 of the Government Code apPlies
only to fi;E;;ial 
interest which Board members may have in
conlracts made by the Agency. This would not appear to apply in
this situatton. 
The Fair Political Practices Act applies only to
contracts in which the public official 
has a material financial
interest. 
As set forth in your statement of facts, Board member
Neufeld has no material financial interest in the contract between
the Agency and the doctors.
Hor.irever, the regulations of the Fair PoIiticaI Practices
Commission provide that the effect of a decision is material if,
"Any person (including a business entity) which has been
a source of income . . . to the official 
of $250 or more
in the preceding 12 months is directly involved in a
decision before the official's 
agency or there is a nexus
[as defined below] between the purpose for which the
official 
receives income and the governmental decision,
. . . There is a nexus between the purpose for which an
official 
receives income and a governmental decision if
the official receives income to achieve a goal or purpose
which would be achieved, defeated, aided, or hindered by
the decision. ,'
Arguably, in this case, Neufeld received income to achieve the goat
of bringing the construction of the building to a point where it
could be occupied. The decision of the Agency to grant the money


John Hendrickson, City I'lanager
January 24, 1991
to the physicians would a.ide in bringing the construction of the
building to a polnt where it could be occupied. Admlttedly this is
a rather broad reading of the regulation. Holrever, I have seen the
rPPC read their reguLations even mole broadly than this'
Slnce, in this case, we are talking about only one member
of the Board being disqualified to act and, if he does not step
dolrn and the FPPC later determines that he should have, possible
criminal penaLties coutd be applied, then I would suggest erring on
the side of caution. Additionally, I assume that Mr. Neufeld has
been paid at least $250 by the doctors i,ithin the last 12 nonths.
Therefore, lf the first part of the above-quoted provlsion is read
separately, then the decision lrhether to grant the money is clearly
material. This analysis wouLd serve further to warant caution.
2l 
Again, can this natter even be acted upon given Dlrector

Short ansverr Yes.

Lhen. the rule of necessity says all of the members of the Board,
inctuding ur. Neufeld, cone back in and can act, so long as they
nake an oral disclosure of their confl-ict of interest before
partlclpating in the decision naking process.
3) 
Are there any other standards or rules the Board ought to be
aware of when confrontlng this type of situation?
Short answeri Yes.
Discussion: As noted above, the Board or the Clty should

Secondly, with this type of issue, different busLnesses or

of interest which may or may not be applicable. For exanple,
building contractors are licensed by the State of California and
are heavily regulated, If they have a code ot professiona.l
conduct, tt may prohibit Director Neufeld fron acting both as

then enough noney to finish out the same project which he he.Lped
buiId.
